rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF LILLIAN WONG
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Relief From Waiver Of Objections To Requests For Admission
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3 M for Relief] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the court is Respondent Ray Torres' Motion for Relief from Waiver of Objections to Request for Admissions. Concurrently set for hearing, is Special Administrator Joyce Wong's Motion for Order That Matters in Requests for Admissions be Deemed Admitted and for Sanctions. The parties do not dispute the Motion was served by email on opposing counsel one day less than the statutory minimum on that service method. There is no showing this late service prejudiced the opposing party; a substantive Opposition to Motion was filed timely. In its discretion, the court will consider the late-served papers. The parties are cautioned to be cognizant of the statutory deadlines for service of documents going forward. Respondent Ray Torres' Motion for Relief from Waiver of Objections to Request for Admissions is GRANTED. Special Administrator Joyce Wong's request for sanctions is DENIED. Under Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.280, If a party to whom requests for admission are directed fails to serve a timely response, the following rules apply: (a) The party to whom the requests for admission are directed waives any objection to the requests, including one based on privilege or on the protection for work product under Chapter 4 (commencing with Section 2018.010). The court, on motion, may relieve that party from this waiver on its determination that both of the following conditions are satisfied: (1) The party has subsequently served a response that is in substantial compliance with Sections 2033.210, 2033.220, and 2033.230. (2) The party's failure to serve a timely response was the result of mistake, inadvertence, or excusable neglect. The Party's Failure to Serve a Timely Response Was the Result of Mistake, Inadvertence, or Excusable Neglect. The Opposition to the Motion argues there is no showing of "mistake, inadvertence, or excusable neglect" on the papers but merely a showing of how a series of failures caused the responses to be late. The court disagrees. The moving papers and supporting declarations show the untimely responses were the result of the responding attorneys' mistake, inadvertence, or excusable neglect. The RFAs were served by email at 4:50 p.m. on a Friday. Staff did not discover the email service until Monday morning, and incorrectly calendared the response deadline as if service was by postal mail and on that Monday. Counsel relied on that incorrect calculation, and did not realize the mistake until seeking an extension days after the deadline had passed (O'Sullivan Decl. at par. 3-4; Loew Decl at par. 3-5). The Reply to Opposition further admits attorney mistake, with colorful colloquialisms: "Ray's attorneys screwed up royally" (Reply at 1:22); "[I]t was his attorneys who screwed up when they did not recognize that they had been served late on the Friday evening." (Reply at 4:2-21); "Ray's attorneys made a mistake, a big one." (Reply at 4:25). The Opposition does not argue, and the court does not find an indication in the record, that the untimeliness was due to something other than the claimed attorney mistake, inadvertence, or excusable neglect, such as consciously choosing to serve late responses, or misunderstanding the relevant statutes.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF LILLIAN WONG
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order That Matters In Requests For Admissions Be Deemed Admitted And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 4 M to Deem Admitted] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the court is Special Administrator Joyce Wong's Motion for Order That Matters in Requests for Admissions be Deemed Admitted and for Sanctions. Concurrently set for hearing, is Respondent Ray Torres' Motion for Relief from Waiver of Objections to Request for Admissions. Special Administrator Joyce Wong's Motion for Order That Matters in Requests for Admissions be Deemed Admitted is DENIED. The request for sanctions is GRANTED. Motion to Deem Truth of the Matter Admitted Under Code of Civil procedure section 2033.280, If a party to whom requests for admission are directed fails to serve a timely response [ ] (b) The requesting party may move for an order that the genuineness of any documents and the truth of any matters specified in the requests be deemed admitted, as well as for a monetary sanction under Chapter 7 (commencing with Section 2023.010). (c) The court shall make this order, unless it finds that the party to whom the requests for admission have been directed has served, before the hearing on the motion, a proposed response to the requests for admission that is in substantial compliance with Section 2033.220. [] The RFAs at issue were served electronically on the evening of August 30, 2024. Timely responses were due by October 1, 2024. Due to a calendaring error disclosed in counsel's declaration in support of the opposition, responding party thought responses were due by October 7, 2024 (Loew Decl. at par. 3). On October 4, 2024, unaware the statutory deadline to respond had passed, responding party's counsel requested a two-week extension to respond. The propounding party counteroffered a one-week extension to October 11, 2024, if objections were waived (O'Sullivan Decl. at par. 4-5; Loew Decl at par. 6, Ex. A email; Granskog Decl. at par. 7, Ex. B email). After further communication between counsel and service of other discovery responses, the propounding party concluded the counteroffer had been rejected (Granskog Decl. at par. 8). On the day the instant Motion was filed, October 11, 2024, the propounding party had not received responses to the RFAs, and a motion under C.C.P. sec. 2033.280(b) appeared to be appropriate. Unbeknownst to the moving party at the time of filing, however, responses to the RFAs had been served by mail the same day, October 11, 2024. They were received by propounding party on October 16, 2024, days after the instant motion had been filed and served (Granskog Decl. at par. 10). The parties do not dispute that responses to the RFAs were served on October 11, 2024, and are untimely. The dispute over whether to grant this Motion lies in whether the untimely responses are "in substantial compliance with Section 2033.220." Under Section 2033.220, (a) Each answer in a response to requests for admission shall be as complete and straightforward as the information reasonably available to the responding party permits. (b) Each answer shall: (1) Admit so much of the matter involved in the request as is true, either as expressed in the request itself or as reasonably and clearly qualified by the responding party. (2) Deny so much of the matter involved in the request as is untrue. (3) Specify so much of the matter involved in the request as to the truth of which the responding party lacks sufficient information or knowledge.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF LILLIAN WONG
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Order That Matters In Requests For Admissions Be Deemed Admitted And For Sanctions.
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 4 M to Deem Admitted] Movant argues in the Reply the responses are not Code-compliant because "the responses contain numerous objections despite the waiver of objections, and the responses did not come close to being "as complete and straightforward as the information reasonably available to the responding party permits" and did not "admit so much of the matter involved in the request as true" as required by Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.220(a) and (b)." (Reply at 5:8-11.) The Granskog declaration at Paragraph 11 states, "The resolution [of the dispute between counsel] would merely have required that Respondent pay the cost Petitioner incurred in the original filing of the motion, and serve amended and compliant responses to requests 25-30 and 33-36." The January 2, 2025 email from attorney Granskog to attorney O'Sullivan, attached as Exhibit E to Granskog's declaration, gives further insight on the argument that the responses to requests 25-30 and 33-36 are not Code-compliant. Beginning at the second paragraph of the email: I have again reviewed the responses you served on October 11, 2024, in response to your email below. While Mr. Torres admitted many of the requests with qualifications, there are also many that he denied. For example, he admitted writing checks 101 and 103 to himself and that he received $3,000 from each check, then qualified his responses by claiming that he spent the money on Lillian's funeral and other expenses directly related to Lillian. However, Mr. Torres denied requests 25-30, each of which references a specific transfer from Lillian Wong's bank account to his own account, and each of which we know he caused. Similarly, Mr. Torres' responses to requests 33-36 likewise denied facts that we all know to be true. Mr. Torres' responses are not code compliant. Cal. Code Civ. Proc. [sec.] 2033.220, subsection (a) requires that "Each answer in a response to requests for admission shall be as complete and straightforward as the information reasonably available to the responding party permits." Subsection (b)(1) states that that each answer shall admit so much of the matter involved in the request as is true, either as expressed in the request itself or as reasonably and clearly qualified by the responding party. Code compliant responses to requests 25-30 would have Mr. Torres admit that he made the transfers, after which admission he is free to qualify the response or add whatever other information he believes is necessary. Likewise, compliant responses to 33-36 would have Mr. Torres admit the facts, then explain why he took the actions referenced if he believes the explanation is important. Granskog Decl., Ex. E.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF LILLIAN WONG
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Relief From Waiver Of Objections To Request For Admissions.
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3 M for Relief] The Party Has Subsequently Served a Response That is in Substantial Compliance with Sections 2033.210, 2033.220, and 2033.230. Section 2033.210 covers form and content of the response. Section 2033.230 covers objections to RFAs. The Opposition does not argue the responses are not in substantial compliance with these code sections. Rather, as in the concurrently set Motion to Deem Admitted, Special Administrator argues the responses are not in substantial compliance with Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.220, because "[t]he responses do not come close to being "as complete and straightforward as the information reasonably available to the responding party permits" and do not "admit so much of the matter involved in the request as is true" as required by Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.220(a) and (b)." (Opp. at 6:20-23.) The responses were served (although late) well before the hearing on this Motion. The case law advises flexibility in determining what qualifies as code-compliant RFA responses, even when the late-responding party is asserting waived objections. "Compliance analysis should prioritize the nature of the substantive answers in the proposed response. While waived objections should be a factor in the assessment, their presence should primarily be addressed through the amount of mandatory monetary sanctions imposed." (See, Katayama v. Cont'l Inv. Grp. (2024) 105 Cal. App. 5th 898, 910.) Here, the responses are verified, they feature a general objection section to all the RFAs and specific objections to most of the RFAs within each response. None of the responses claim an inability to answer. Each response is either Admit or Deny, with a short qualification to most responses. Although a denial of all or any portion of a request for admission must be unequivocal, reasonable qualifications are not improper. (St. Mary v. Superior Court (2014) 223 Cal. App. 4th 762, 780.) If the objections are ignored, each response is substantive and meaningful. Although the responses have not actually complied with all statutory requirements, "such actual compliance is not required where the proposed response is facially a good-faith effort to respond to RFAs in a manner that is substantially code-compliant." (St. Mary v. Superior Ct. (2014) 223 Cal. App. 4th 762, 780-82.) For the purposes of this Motion, the court finds the late responses are "in substantial compliance with Sections 2033.210, 2033.220, and 2033.230."

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF LILLIAN WONG
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Order That Matters In Requests For Admissions Be Deemed Admitted And For Sanctions.
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 4 M to Deem Admitted] The case law on motions to deem admitted brought under section 2033.280 does not discuss whether "in substantial compliance with Section 2033.220" must be synonymous with "objectively true" or even "what the propounding party thinks is true." If, having received the substantially compliant responses, the propounding party deems that an answer to a particular request is evasive or incomplete, and/or an objection to a particular request is without merit or too general, the remedy is to bring a motion to compel further responses under Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.290. As discussed in St. Mary v. Superior Ct. (2014) 223 Cal. App. 4th 762, 783-84, it would be an abuse of discretion for the court to misapply a section 2033.280 motion to deem admitted, to effectively grant a motion to compel further responses under section 2033.290 that has not been filed. Sanctions Under Code of Civil Proceeding section 2033.280(c), "It is mandatory that the court impose a monetary sanction under Chapter 7 (commencing with Section 2023.010) on the party or attorney, or both, whose failure to serve a timely response to requests for admission necessitated this motion." Ciaran O'Sullivan and Jeffery R. Loew, the attorneys for Respondent Ray Torres, jointly and severally, are ordered to pay Special Administrator Joyce L. Wong sanctions on this Motion in the amount of two thousand and thirty-five dollars ($2,035), within 10 days of service of notice of entry of this order.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF DONALD RAY FUNKHOUSER
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 5/6/25. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) Notice to a minor must be given directly to the minor and to the person having legal custody of the minor with whom the minor resides. [CRC 7.51(d).] (2) Distribution to a minor cannot be made UNTIL a guardian of the minor's estate has been appointed or the proposed distribution is to be into a blocked account at a specific bank. [Please read LRSF 14.35 E.] (3) Administrator alleges in paragraph 18 of his second amended petition that he did not hire any person who has a relationship to him. He is seeking reimbursement for travel expenses for his daughter and son-in-law to assist him with his trip to California. (4) Administrator has kept well in excess of the FDIC insured amount in one bank account. Deadline is 4/22/25 for providing the assigned examiner courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues. (Please do not file another fully amended petition.] Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B. NB: Item 4 of an I&A should NOT be completed when a probate referee HAS BEEN appointed. A petitioner does not need to give notice to himself. [Probate Code Section 1201.]

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Compliance Status Hearing For Duane
rulings.Rulings: Hearing. Appearance required. Please have the CASE NUMBER and the CASE NAME ready for the clerk. Parties appearing must check in for the hearing using their FULL NAME. Counsel and parties may appear in person or remotely through Zoom or CourtCall. Please review LRSF 14.1 D. for information on remote appearances. For Zoom appearance: meeting ID is 160 225 4765 and password is 514879. For audio-only telephone appearance via Zoom, call 1-669-254-5252 and follow the prompts, using the meeting ID and password above. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling 1-888-882-6878.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF ERLINDA MODALES LABAY
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION TO TERMINATE ESTATE (NO ASSETS)
rulings.Rulings: Grant without hearing. (The attorney's "Response" filed on 12/23/24 will be disregarded. A Court-appointed fiduciary is required to sign and verify his own pleadings, including any supplement or amendment thereto. [CRC 7.103(c) and Probate Code Section 1023.] Defective notice is corrected simply by filing proof of service of the correct notice, not by describing that it will be done.)

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE AUSTIN EDWARD HILLS 2004 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition (1) To Confirm Validity Of The First Amendment; (2) To Compel Accounting And Production Of Trust Records; (3) For Redress Of Breach Of Trust And Surcharge; And (4) For An Order Enjoining Respondent From Using Trust Assets
rulings.Rulings: Appearance required. Issues to include: 1) No objection has been filed (on the amended petition). 2) Direct notice was not given on Justin Hills. Remote appearances may be made. Please review Local Rule 14.1. The following information pertains to appearances by Zoom: WHEN ENTERING "YOUR NAME" ON ZOOM, BOTH THE FIRST AND LAST NAME, AS WELL THE CASE NUMBER, MUST BE INCLUDED. Meeting ID: 160 225 4765 Password: 514879 To appear by telephone through Zoom, call 1-669-254-5252, and follow the prompts, using the meeting ID and password above.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE AUSTIN EDWARD HILLS 2004 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Justin Hills Cross-Petition For: 1)Confirmation Of Justin Hills' Role As Successor Trutee. 2)Delivery Of All Trust Property To Successor Trustee. 3)Redress Of Breach Of Fiduciary Duty. 4)Return Of Turst Property Pursuant To Probate Code 850. 5)Preliminary And Permanent Injuction Preventing Cross-Respondent Austin Hills And Others From Interfering With The Management Of Dos Colinas Estate. 6)Declaratory Relief Regarding Attorney'S Fees
rulings.Rulings: Appearance required. Issues to include: Notice of Justin Hills' cross petition, which includes relief under Probate Code 850, was not provided on the correct Judicial Council Form (DE-115). NB: personal service is also required under Probate Code 17203(b). Remote appearances may be made. Please review Local Rule 14.1. The following information pertains to appearances by Zoom: WHEN ENTERING "YOUR NAME" ON ZOOM, BOTH THE FIRST AND LAST NAME, AS WELL THE CASE NUMBER, MUST BE INCLUDED. Meeting ID: 160 225 4765 Password: 514879 To appear by telephone through Zoom, call 1-669-254-5252, and follow the prompts, using the meeting ID and password above.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF THOMAS MARTIN HAMILTON
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION
rulings.Rulings: Granted per written order. Status of closing the estate will be set for 2/17/26. The administrator must file either a petition for final distribution or a petition for approval of report of status of administration pursuant to Prob. C. Section 12200 (requesting additional time for estate administration) at least two weeks before 2/17/26. Appearance will be required on that status date if no petition has been filed.
